original_hypothesis.title: PermissionValidator Out-of-Bounds Calldata Read Bypasses Session Permission Rules
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: PermissionValidator.validatePermission reads call.data at rule.offset using LibBytes.readBytes32, which does not check if the offset is within the call.data slice bounds. calldataload reads from the raw transaction calldata, not just the slice. An attacker can craft call.data shorter than the required offset, so the PermissionValidator reads adjacent calldata (from other payload fields or signature data) that satisfies the permission rule, while the target contract receiving the actual call.data would see zero-padded bytes at that offset. This discrepancy allows bypassing permission constraints on session keys.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: LibBytes.readBytes32 uses raw calldataload without bounds checking. The calldata slice for call.data is a bounded view, but calldataload ignores slice boundaries. The attacker controls the full calldata layout and can position satisfying bytes at the OOB read location. This is a concrete, exploitable discrepancy between validation-time and execution-time data views.

proof_of_signal.pattern: call.data.readBytes32(rule.offset) uses calldataload without checking rule.offset < call.data.length; adjacent calldata readable

preconditions: Wallet uses SessionManager with explicit session permissions containing parameter rules
Permission rule has rule.offset that can exceed actual call.data length
Session key holder crafts payload with short call.data and positions matching bytes in adjacent calldata

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: calldataload at arbitrary offset within calldata slice without bounds check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: readBytes32 does calldataload(add(_pointer, _data.offset)) regardless of _data.length; reads adjacent calldata when _pointer >= _data.length

analysis_questions: Can the attacker reliably position specific bytes at the OOB read offset in the SessionManager calldata?
What permission rule configurations are most exploitable?
Does the ABI encoding layout make precise positioning feasible?
validation_requirements: Construct a PoC where call.data is shorter than rule.offset but adjacent calldata satisfies the rule
Verify the target contract receives only call.data (zero-padded) at the same offset
Demonstrate a concrete permission bypass scenario

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Session key with permission rule checking offset 100 = VALUE. Attacker sends call.data of 36 bytes. At calldata position call.data.offset+100, attacker places VALUE (from another call's data or signature bytes). PermissionValidator sees VALUE and passes. Target contract at offset 100 sees bytes32(0).
prerequisites: Parameter rule offset exceeds minimum call.data length needed for the actual target function

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Session key can bypass all parameter-level permission restrictions, potentially executing unauthorized token transfers or contract calls beyond intended limits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All wallets using explicit session keys with parameter rules
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Revoke session key or update wallet config

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for OOB offset reads in permission validation with short call.data

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing call.data.length vs rule.offset in transaction trace

validation_result.mechanism: OOB calldata read in permission validation
validation_result.impact_summary: PermissionValidator.validatePermission reads call.data at arbitrary offsets via LibBytes.readBytes32 without bounds checking, allowing session key holders to bypass parameter-level permission rules by crafting short call.data while positioning satisfying bytes in adjacent calldata.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: LibBytes.readBytes32 uses raw `calldataload(add(_pointer, _data.offset))` which does not respect the bounds of the calldata slice `_data`. When `rule.offset` exceeds `call.data.length`, the read leaks into adjacent ABI-encoded fields in the SessionManager's incoming calldata. The attacker (session key holder) controls the signature data and payload structure, which are adjacent in the calldata. During actual execution, `Calls._execute` passes `call.data` (bounded memory bytes) to the target contract via `LibOptim.call`. The target contract receives only the exact bytes of `call.data`; any `calldataload` beyond its length returns zero. This creates a concrete discrepancy: PermissionValidator sees attacker-controlled adjacent bytes at the OOB offset, while the target contract sees zeros. A permission rule checking e.g. EQUAL at an offset beyond call.data.length could be satisfied by positioning the expected value in the adjacent calldata (from signature or other encoded fields), while the target function executes with zero-valued parameters at that position.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: LibBytes.sol explicitly documents this behavior: '@dev These functions do not check if the input index is within the bounds of the data array. @dev Reading out of bounds may return dirty values.' PermissionValidator.sol line ~72 calls `call.data.readBytes32(rule.offset)` without any prior check that `rule.offset + 32 <= call.data.length`. The assembly in readBytes32 computes `calldataload(add(_pointer, _data.offset))` which reads raw transaction calldata regardless of slice bounds.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: calldataload reads 32 bytes starting at `_pointer + _data.offset` from raw transaction calldata. No check ensures `_pointer + 32 <= _data.length`. When `_pointer >= _data.length`, adjacent calldata (from other ABI-encoded fields) is read instead.

Reads call.data at rule.offset without checking that rule.offset + 32 <= call.data.length. If rule.offset exceeds call.data.length, readBytes32 reads adjacent calldata fields, not the actual call data that will be sent to the target contract.

calling_sequence: 1. BaseSig.recoverBranch encounters FLAG_SIGNATURE_SAPIENT for SessionManager, calls ISapient(addr).recoverSapientSignature(payload, signature) - this ABI-encodes the payload into a new external call
2. SessionManager.recoverSapientSignature decodes signature via SessionSig.recoverSignature, iterates calls
3. For explicit calls, calls _validateExplicitCall(payload, callIdx, wallet, sessionSigner, ...)
4. ExplicitSessionManager._validateExplicitCall finds the matching permission, calls validatePermission(permission, call, wallet, signer, limits)
6. LibBytes.readBytes32 executes calldataload(add(rule.offset, call.data.offset)) - if rule.offset > call.data.length, reads adjacent ABI-encoded calldata
7. Later, Calls._execute sends call.data (memory-bounded bytes) to target contract via LibOptim.call
8. Target contract receives only the bounded call.data; calldataload beyond its length returns 0
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The validation path through SessionManager -> ExplicitSessionManager._validateExplicitCall -> PermissionValidator.validatePermission reads calldata at arbitrary offsets without bounds checking. The execution path through Calls._execute -> LibOptim.call sends only the bounded call.data to the target. This creates a validation/execution discrepancy when rule.offset exceeds call.data.length.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: SessionManager is deployed without constructor arguments per Deploy.s.sol. The vulnerability is in the code logic, not deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A session key holder has permission with a rule checking offset 100 for EQUAL to value V. The attacker crafts call.data of 4 bytes (just a function selector). When PermissionValidator reads at offset 100, `calldataload(call.data.offset + 100)` reads bytes from the ABI-encoded signature or other payload fields in SessionManager's calldata. The attacker positions value V at that location by carefully structuring the encodedSignature. PermissionValidator sees V and passes. The target contract receives only 4 bytes of calldata; any parameter access at offset 100 yields zeros. The attacker has bypassed the parameter constraint.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Session key holders can bypass parameter-level permission rules set by wallet owners. This could allow unauthorized token transfers, exceeding spending limits encoded in parameter rules, or calling target contract functions with unintended parameter values. The severity depends on the specific permission configurations but affects all wallets using explicit sessions with parameter rules.
recommended_mitigations: Add bounds check in PermissionValidator.validatePermission before readBytes32: require(rule.offset + 32 <= call.data.length, 'Rule offset out of bounds')
Alternatively, modify LibBytes.readBytes32 to revert when _pointer + 32 > _data.length
Consider adding a safe variant of readBytes32 that returns (bytes32, bool) indicating whether the read was in-bounds
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy SessionManager and a mock target contract. 2. Configure a wallet with a session key having a permission rule checking offset 100 EQUAL to 0xABCD... 3. Craft a transaction with call.data of only 4 bytes (selector). 4. Structure the encodedSignature so that the ABI-encoded calldata at position call.data.offset+100 contains 0xABCD... 5. Submit the transaction and verify validatePermission passes. 6. Verify the target contract receives only 4 bytes and reads zeros at offset 100.
uncertainties: The exact byte positioning of adjacent calldata depends on ABI encoding layout of the SessionManager's recoverSapientSignature call, which needs careful calculation for a working exploit
Some permission configurations may not have rules with offsets that could exceed typical call.data lengths, reducing practical impact for those specific cases
additional_testing_needed: Construct a full PoC demonstrating the OOB read with specific ABI encoding layout calculations
Test with various rule offset values and call.data lengths to map the exploitable parameter space
Verify that the ABI encoding of the external call to SessionManager allows reliable positioning of target bytes
